artifacts.content:
Hi Izzy,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m staring at three different color‑coded calendars trying to make April make sense. I figured I’d write you instead of doom‑scrolling.

“Last Light Over the Mesa” is officially locked for April 14–18, with that backup day on the 25th. We’ll be out on those BLM-managed public lands about 25–40 miles west of town, same kind of high‑desert mesas you and I used to hike with Papá—open scrub, scattered juniper, big sunrise/sunset horizons. It’s honestly gorgeous, and the director is obsessed with that distant mountain view. I keep thinking about the turnout off County Road 279 where we scattered Papá Jorge’s ashes in October 2019 and nicknamed it “Jorge Vista Plateau.” Part of me still wants to steal one shot from that exact ridge.

Work pressure is…a lot. High Desert Horizon Productions fell 90 days behind on vendor payments last fall, and I ended up negotiating that $18,000 payment plan with Rio Grande Grip & Electric in October 2023. Because of that, Luis has me personally supervising every equipment delivery and signing all the BLM check‑in sheets at the Rio Puerco filming area. Add that to being the line producer and location manager and “person responsible on location,” and I feel like I’m spinning ten plates.

On top of it, I’m still spooked from the panic attack I had on set outside Gallup on March 18, 2024. Dr. Karen Holbrook at Presbyterian Rust in Rio Rancho wants me capping on‑site hours at eight, but you know how these days creep. I’m trying to be disciplined so I don’t end up back in her office shaking.

Anyway, kids are good—Lucia’s desert plants project has basically turned our balcony into a mini‑mesa, and Mateo keeps stealing my walkies. Call this weekend?

Besos,
Elena

metadata.subject: April shoot + my brain on overload

artifacts.content:
Hi Carlos,

I know we already talked in person, but I wanted to write this out so we’re on the same page before April turns into chaos.

For the mesa shoot, I’ll still be listed as the person responsible on location, but I get why you’re nervous after my DUI arrest driving back from that scout near Santa Fe on July 2, 2022. I haven’t forgotten sitting on the side of the road with the trooper lecturing me, or how long it took to get past the shame. If you and Michael Ortega want his info down as an emergency contact and don’t want me signing anything solo tied to vehicle use or risk management, I can live with that. It’s annoying, but fair.

I’m already trying to build in guardrails. After the panic attack on set outside Gallup on March 18, 2024, Dr. Holbrook at Presbyterian Rust basically ordered me to keep on‑site days to eight hours max. No more 14‑hour hero days. Between that and Dr. Elena Chavez at UNM Behavioral Health telling me not to work past 20:00 on high‑stress shoots after the October 2024 incident, I feel like I have a whole committee in my head.

I’m also thinking about hours because of the kids. On the days Lucia has her 19:30 telehealth sessions with Dr. Karen Holt at Sandia Children’s Behavioral Health, I have to be home by 19:00 to do her ADHD meds and log‑in with her. That March 2025 custody agreement is crystal clear about that.

I don’t want work, or my past screw‑ups, to mess with any of that. We’ll keep the vehicles boring and the days sane.

– E

Personal planning – family / work / money

1. High Desert Horizon finances
- Remember: New Mexico Taxation & Revenue filed that $47,800 lien against High Desert Horizon Productions, LLC in Santa Fe on 2/12/24.
- In March 2024 budgets I literally labeled the project “Last Light Over the Mesa: A High Desert Horizon Productions Tax Lien Recovery Project.” Dark humor, but also true.
- Meeting with Mark Ellison (now wearing the bankruptcy attorney hat, not just “Dad’s old BLM supervisor” from 2003) on Zoom 2026‑04‑23 at 20:00 to go over the Chapter 13 repayment plan for back payroll taxes. Hard stop on set at 19:30 that day.

2. Family legal stuff
- Bernalillo County Family Court mediation 1/9/24 still hanging over everything. I started calling the current edit “Last Light Over the Mesa – Lucia & Mateo Custody Cut” in my January 2024 notebook after Carlos’s attorney pushed to trim the more personal kid scenes.
- Custody agreement from March 2025: be home on Lucia’s telehealth nights (ADHD meds + 19:30 Sandia Children’s Behavioral Health sessions with Dr. Holt).

3. Dad history woven into film
- Story still rooted in reporting Papá Jorge’s drinking as a seasonal ranger at Petroglyph in May 2003 → confrontation at Albuquerque BLM field office 6/12/03 with his supervisor Mark Ellison → forced early retirement, estrangement until his 2019 death.
- Keep asking myself how much of that I can keep without feeling like I’m re‑litigating our whole family in public.

Breathe. One thing at a time.

metadata.title: Notebook entry – threads to keep straight

artifacts.content:
Event: Ojito Wilderness shoot week – logistics + kids
Date: May 13–17, 2024
Time: 06:30–18:30 daily
Location: Base camp near BLM Ojito Wilderness access road
Attendees: Me; crew; Ana (with kids)

Notes:
- I’m the person responsible on location for this one, but because of the temporary custody arrangement Carlos and I signed in Bernalillo County Family Court in March 2024, Mom (Ana Delgado) will physically be at base camp with Lucia and Mateo on school days.
- Build in daily 20‑minute safety walk‑through at call time (07:00–07:20) after the November 9, 2023 workers’ comp claim from the PA’s ankle sprain on uneven BLM terrain near San Ysidro. Rachel Kim at Sandia Mutual wants that documented with sign‑in sheets.
- No days longer than 8 hours on‑site for me; still honoring Dr. Holbrook’s recommendation after the March 18, 2024 panic attack outside Gallup. Let Daniel cover any late load‑outs.
- Keep hours no later than 18:30 so I can get home, reset, and not flirt with the 20:00 cutoff Dr. Chavez set after the October 2024 panic episode.
- Remind Lucia’s teacher about absences; flag that she’ll still need time for her 19:30 Sandia Children’s Behavioral Health telehealth with Dr. Holt on Wednesday even if we’re wrapped.

To bring: kids’ homework packets, Lucia’s desert plants notebook, Mateo’s headphones + tablet, extra snacks for Mom (watch her blood sugar; remember the June 14, 2023 hypoglycemic scare at the county road/BLM pullout).

metadata.title: Ojito shoot – master calendar entry
metadata.location: Ojito Wilderness access road base camp

artifacts.content:
Hi Diego,

Can’t sleep, so of course I’m story‑boarding in my head and thinking about you.

I went back out this weekend to the BLM plateau just north of where you were camped in your car last winter, off that dirt road about 40 miles west of Albuquerque. December 2024–January 2025 is burned into my brain from all those weekends bringing you food. I know every washboard section, every safe pullout, where the gas is, and exactly where the cell coverage dies. It’s weirdly comforting and heartbreaking at the same time.

The spot really is perfect for “Last Light Over the Mesa”: open 2–3 acre turnout, distant mountains, scattered juniper. It reminds me a lot of the low mesa where we quietly scattered a portion of Papá Jorge’s ashes on May 5, 2020 after his COVID funeral at Garcia Mortuary. The little line of stones we set facing his favorite western ridgeline is still there. I keep promising myself if we shoot there I’ll make sure nothing and no one disturbs that memorial.

The story keeps drifting closer to our real mess. The climactic argument is basically my own March 17, 2014 protective‑order day at Bernalillo County District Court—when I filed for the TRO after Papá showed up drunk and threatening at my Silver Ave apartment while Lucia slept in the next room. And then there’s the graveside monologue, which is just me at San Jose de Armijo on November 2, 2022, finally reading the letter about the $18,000 personal loan he took out in my name in 2017, the one the collections agency blindsided me with in March 2021.

I don’t know if turning all of that into a movie is healing or just picking at a scab. But standing on that plateau where you were trying to survive in your car, it felt like maybe we could turn at least one hard place into something beautiful.

Love you,
Lena

metadata.subject: Your plateau, Papá, and this film

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just wanted to write everything down so you don’t feel lost in all the moving pieces.

For the April 14–18 mesa shoot, we’ll be on BLM-managed public lands roughly 25–40 miles west of Albuquerque—same kind of terrain as the gravel pullout where you had the hypoglycemic episode on June 14, 2023 and we waited ~45 minutes for the UNM ambulance. I actually like using that disturbed gravel pullout at the county road/BLM two‑track junction as base camp now, because I *know* emergency vehicles can get in and out, and it fits crew parking, a 10x20 catering tent, and a first‑aid station.

You don’t have to worry about long days alone. Our hours of use are 06:00–19:30. I built that around needing to be back near Presbyterian by 20:15 so I can give you your 20:30 insulin shot at the apartment. On the nights Isabel can cover, maybe I’ll stay a little longer, but I promised Dr. Liu at Presbyterian Medical Group I’d respect my own limits too—100 mg sertraline every morning since October 2021 for the panic disorder only does so much if I keep pushing.

Insurance is breathing down my neck since the November 9, 2023 workers’ comp claim when the PA sprained his ankle on the San Ysidro BLM terrain. Rachel Kim at Sandia Mutual now requires that I personally lead a 20‑minute safety walk‑through at call time each day. So picture me at sunrise, coffee in one hand, clipboard in the other, lecturing a bunch of sleepy grips about rocks and gopher holes.

It’s a lot, but I’m trying to design this whole thing—work and home—so nobody ends up in the ER.

Te quiero,
Elena

metadata.subject: April shoot, your insulin, and safety stuff

artifacts.content:
Project + home to‑dos – “Last Light Over the Mesa”

Locations
- Primary filming area: high‑desert mesa turnout ~30 miles west of ABQ, similar to the BLM tract off County Road 279 where Carlos and I scattered Papá Jorge’s ashes in Oct 2019 (“Jorge Vista Plateau”). Need updated GPS pins.
- Parking/base camp: disturbed gravel pullout at junction of county road and BLM two‑track; we staged about 12 crew vehicles + 10x20 catering tent there on 11/19/21 night shoot when PA filed HR complaint about gaffer’s gross comments. Good capacity, but heavy emotional baggage.

Schedule / hours
- Standard days: 06:00–19:30. No night filming. After October 2024 panic attack on set, Dr. Elena Chavez at UNM Behavioral Health said no work past 20:00 during high‑stress shoots.
- 2026‑04‑24: shorten to 08:00–16:00 so I can drive Mom and be at Bernalillo County Metro Court by 17:00 for Diego’s DUI sentencing.

Family / kids
- Lucia: keep track of 19:30 Sandia Children’s Behavioral Health telehealth sessions with Dr. Holt (ADHD meds at 19:00 sharp). Custody agreement from March 2025 means no “sorry, we’re in overtime” excuses.
- Mateo: equine OT with the mini‑donkey at New Mexico Equine Rehab & Wellness Center started Jan 2024; check with Sarah (OT) before I even think about asking if that same therapy mini‑donkey can appear in a scene.

Company / money
- Still on $18,000 payment plan with Rio Grande Grip & Electric from October 2023 (90 days behind last fall). I need to be physically present for all their truck arrivals and sign every check‑in sheet.

Remember: film is important, but nobody’s freedom, health, or sobriety is worth a cool shot.

metadata.title: Master checklist – don’t drop any balls
